description: A proof that works with atoms works with anything.

guides.text: The goal is $(A \land B) \to (A \land B)$. You could prove this from scratch — assume the conjunction, then discharge. Three steps. Or you could prove it in **two** — by using a proof you already have and substituting.

The **substitution rule** says: if a judgement holds with some atoms, it also holds when you uniformly replace those atoms with arbitrary formulas. Formally, $\Gamma \vdash p$ implies $\Gamma[P:=F] \vdash p[P:=F]$ for any $P$ and $F$. First, prove the identity $P \to P$ — a proof we've seen since problem 01.
Step 2 proves $P \to P$. Now substitute $P$ everywhere with $(A \land B)$. The syntax: `(subst N (P (and A B)))` — the step number, then pairs of `(atom replacement)`.

Step 3 proves $(A \land B) \to (A \land B)$ — exactly the goal, reached in two proof steps plus one substitution. The key insight: **substitution is a structural rule**. The shape of a correct proof doesn't depend on the particular atoms — replace them with anything and the reasoning still holds.

unlocks.name: subst
unlocks.description: Uniformly replace atoms with formulas in a proven judgement. The proof structure is independent of the particular symbols — a structural rule.
